metadata.title: The Verified Data Folder: Prove Your Inputs Are Clean
metadata.playlist: Build the Recipe — Make Your Tool Conductor-Ready
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 5B brief — Nik's Recipe-Based Framework

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 2 of 6. The verified data folder is not a place you dump everything you pulled from the API. It is proof. Proof that your inputs exist, are clean, and passed your own quality gate. The gate you wrote in the brand_config.json quality_gate field — that is the rule every record in this folder had to survive to be here. Twenty-five verified records per source, a README documenting the gate and the count, and — and this is the honesty beat — a record of how many records failed and why. Hiding rejections is not graded. Documenting them is. Let's go.

props.segment: The Verified Data Folder: Prove Your Inputs Are Clean
props.command: Build data/verified/: 25+ records per source, README with gate + count + rejections.
props.runningText: the GIGO gate from the config is the rule every record must survive…
output: README: record count · last update · gate applied · rejections + why

beats.act: folder structure and the gigo gate
beats.narration_text: The verified folder lives at data/verified/ inside your project directory. Under it, one subfolder for each data source you named in the brand_config.json data_sources array. If you have three sources — a news RSS feed, a Kaggle dataset, and a YouTube API pull — you have three subfolders: data/verified/news_feed/, data/verified/kaggle_articles/, data/verified/youtube_transcripts/. The names should match the local_path values you put in the config. Each subfolder contains the records that passed the GIGO gate. GIGO stands for garbage in, garbage out. The gate is the quality_gate rule from the config: a record must have a title, a date in YYYY-MM-DD, and a source name. A record missing any required field does not go in data/verified/ — it goes nowhere, or it goes in a rejected/ subfolder you document in the README. Raw API responses, full JSON dumps, or unvalidated files do not belong in data/verified/. They belong in data/raw/ if you keep them at all.

artifactLines: data/verified/<source_name>/ — one subfolder per config data_source.
Subfolder names match local_path values in the config.
Contents: records that passed the quality_gate rule from the config — and only those.
Raw API responses or unvalidated files → data/raw/ or rejected/ — not here.
GIGO: a record missing a required field does not enter verified/.
props.sparkLine: Passed the gate → data/verified/. Everything else → out.

beats.act: the readme and the honesty beat
beats.narration_text: The README.md in data/verified/ has one section per source subfolder. Each section has four things. Record count: how many records are currently in this subfolder. Last update date: when the records were last pulled and verified. Quality gate applied: copy the exact quality_gate text from the config. And rejection summary: how many records from the raw pull failed the gate and why. The rejection summary is the honesty beat. It is easy to document twenty-five passing records and say nothing about the thirty that did not pass. But a rejection summary that says 'twelve records failed: nine were missing a date field, three had duplicate titles with existing records' tells the grader two things — that you actually applied the gate, and that you understand why records fail. That is graded. An empty rejection section, or one that says 'none,' when a data pull of any meaningful size would have some failures, signals that the gate was not applied rigorously. Document the rejections. Include the count and the reason.

artifactLines: Record count: how many records are in this subfolder.
Last update date: when records were last pulled and verified.
Quality gate applied: exact quality_gate text from the config.
Rejection summary: how many failed the gate + why (missing field / duplicate / off-topic).
Empty rejections = gate not applied rigorously. Document the count and the reason.
props.sparkLine: Count. Gate. Rejections + why. Honesty is graded.

beats.narration_text: Four traps that cost Part 2 points. Trap one: raw dumps. If data/verified/ contains a full API response JSON with every field the API returned, it is not a verified folder — it is a raw dump with a verified label. Each record should contain only the fields that the quality gate checked. Trap two: no README. Without the README, there is no documentation that the gate was applied. Trap three: records that never passed the gate. If your quality gate requires a date in YYYY-MM-DD and a verified record has a date in a different format or no date at all, the gate was either not applied or was applied incorrectly. Open your records and check them against the gate rule before submitting. Trap four: hiding rejections. A rejection section that says zero when you pulled five hundred records and admitted twenty-five signals that either the gate was not applied, or you removed the records that failed without counting them. If you genuinely had zero rejections from a large pull, say so explicitly and explain why — that is an unusual result and the grader will notice.

artifactLines: Trap 1 — Raw dumps: full API response in verified/ = not verified. Only gate-checked fields.
Trap 2 — No README: no documentation the gate was applied.
Trap 3 — Records that never passed: open records and check against gate rule before submitting.
Trap 4 — Hidden rejections: 0 rejections from a large pull = unusual. Explain it explicitly or document the count.
props.sparkLine: Gate-checked fields only. README. Real rejections documented.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for the verified data folder. One subfolder per source, names matching the local_path values in the config. Twenty-five or more records per subfolder, cleaned and deduplicated, every record passing the quality_gate rule from the config. README.md with four items per source: record count, last update date, quality gate applied, and rejection summary with count and reason. No raw dumps. No unvalidated records. No hidden rejections. The GIGO gate is the rule — only records that survive it belong in data/verified/. The rejection summary is not optional — it is the evidence that you applied the gate rather than just labeled a folder. Episode 3 explains the four-layer architecture and the five judgment types before you write the recipe.

props.artifactTitle: The Verified Data Folder: Prove Your Inputs Are Clean
artifactLines: One subfolder per source, names matching config local_path values.
25+ records per subfolder — cleaned, deduped, gate-passing.
README: record count · last update · quality gate text · rejection summary (count + reason).
No raw dumps. No unvalidated records.
Rejection summary is evidence of gate application — not optional.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude to draft the README from the data you have. The prompt reads: I am writing the README.md for a data/verified/ folder in an n8n-based Madison tool. I have three data sources. For each, I will give you the source name, the quality gate rule, the count of records that passed, the count of records that failed, and the primary reason they failed. Source 1: [name], gate: [paste quality_gate from config], passed: [number], failed: [number], reason: [description]. Source 2: [same structure]. Source 3: [same structure]. Write the README.md with one section per source using exactly these four headings: Record Count, Last Update Date, Quality Gate Applied, and Rejection Summary. For each rejection summary, write one sentence naming the count and the primary failure reason. Do not invent a failure reason — if I gave you 'missing date field,' use exactly that phrase. Take the README, open the actual record files in data/verified/, and verify that the record count in the README matches the actual file count. If they do not match, fix the README before submitting.
